turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kennedy has the opinion the Court this morning in case 15-606, Pena-Rodriguez versus Colorado.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: After trial by a jury in a Colorado state court, the defendant, who is now the petitioner in this case, was convicted of criminal charges arising from his alleged sexual assault of two teenage girls. After the judgment of conviction was entered and the jury was discharged, almost at once, two jurors approached defense counsel. They said that during the jury deliberations, another juror, now called H.C., had expressed anti-Hispanic bias toward the defendant and the defendant's alibi witnesses. With the trial court's supervision, the defense counsel obtained affidavits from those two jurors. The affidavits described a number of H.C.'s statements, and in one comment during the jury deliberations, H.C., again referring to the defendant and the petitioner, said, “I think he did it because he's Mexican. And Mexican men take whatever they want.” The state trial court acknowledged that the statement indicated H.C.'s apparent bias but it denied the motion for a new trial. In declining to rely on the evidence of the statements that were made during the jury deliberations, the trial court cited Colorado Rule of Evidence 606(b). Now, the Colorado rule is the counterpart of a parallel federal rule, Federal Rule of Evidence 606(b) and these rules do generally prohibit a juror from testifying as to any statement made during deliberations in any later proceeding inquiring into the validity of the verdict and this is known as the No-impeachment Rule, the verdict cannot be impeached. The Colorado Court of Appeals and the Colorado Supreme Court both agreed with the state court that, under the state rule, the affidavits about what H.C. said could not be admitted in evidence to impeach the verdict. This Court granted review to decide whether there is a constitutional exception to the No-impeachment Rule when racial bias is at issue. To explain the background and reasons for Colorado's rule barring testimony from jurors about their deliberations, today's opinion explains the rule's Common Law origins and its later development in this country. Some version of the No-impeachment Rule is followed throughout the nation. Most jurisdictions have adopted a variation or a close parallel to the Federal Rule. At least 16 jurisdictions and one federal court of appeals have made an exception to the No-impeachment Rule for juror testimony that racial bias played a role in deliberations. Now, since the enactment of Federal Rule 606(b), this Court has addressed whether the Constitution mandates an exception to the No-impeachment Rule in two different cases. And in both of those cases, this Court declined to find an exception. The first case is Tanner versus Unite States. There, a juror was prepared to testify that some jurors were under the influence of drugs and alcohol during the trial. The Tanner Court did not create an exception to the rule barring juror testimony about the deliberations, so the testimony could not be considered. The Tanner Court explained that that, as a general matter, the No-impeachment Rule has substantial merit. It promotes full and vigorous discussion by the jurors and it provides assurance that after being discharged, the jurors will not be summoned to recount their deliberations or be otherwise harassed. So the rule helped to preserve the finality and stability of jury verdicts. The second relevant case is Warger versus Shauers. There, this Court once again rejected an exception to the No-Impeachment Rule. In Warger, testimony from a jury was offered after the trial to show that the jury forewoman, during deliberations, had expressed the pro-defendant bias that she had concealed during the voir dire. While the Court did not permit an exception to the bar on juror testimony in that case, it did say that there may be need for an exception in the case of juror bias so extreme that almost by definition the jury trial right has been abridged. Today, the Court must ask whether as a constitutional matter that exception is applicable here. It must become the heritage of our nation to rise above racial classifications that are so inconsistent with our commitment to the equal dignity of all persons. And this imperative to purge racial prejudice from the administration of justice was given new force and direction by the ratification of the Civil War Amendments to the Constitution. Time and again, this Court has been called upon to enforce the Constitution's guarantee against state-sponsored racial discrimination in the jury system. The unmistakable principle of these precedents is the discrimination on the basis of races especially pernicious in the administration of justice. It damages both the fact and the perception of the jury's rule as a vital check against the wrongful exercise of power by the state. So, today's case lights up the entered section of two lines of this Court's decisions; one endorsing the No-Impeachment Rule, the other is seeking to eliminate racial bias in the jury system. Those lines of precedent need not conflict. Racial bias, unlike the conduct at issue in Tanner or Warger, implicates unique historical, constitutional, and institutional concerns. Racial bias is a familiar and recurring evil. If left unaddressed, it risks systemic injury to the Administration of Justice. For these and other reasons, the Court now holds that where a juror makes a clear statement that indicates he or she relied on a racial stereotype or animus to convict a criminal defendant, the Sixth Amendment requires that the No-Impeachment Rule give way. This will permit the trial court to consider the evidence of the juror's statement in any resulting denial of the jury right. Not every offhand comment indicating racial bias or hostility will justify setting aside the No-Impeachment bar. There must be a threshold showing that one and more of the jurors made statements exhibiting overt racial bias, statements that cast serious doubt on the fairness and impartiality of the jury's deliberation and resulting verdict. This Court is confident that the experience of the jurisdictions that already recognize a racial bias exception and the experience of the courts going forward will inform the proper exercise of the trial court's discretion in these and related matters. The nation must continue to make strides to overcome race-based discrimination. And it is the mark of a maturing legal system that it seeks to understand and implement these lessons of history. For these reasons, the judgment of the Colorado Supreme Court is reversed. The case is remanded for further proceedings not inconsistent with the Court's opinion. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion. Justice Alito has filed a dissenting opinion in which the Chief Justice and Justice Thomas have joined.
